subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around wherever you are sitting right now. If you are near a torch, a mobile phone, a radio, or a solar lamp charging by the window, you are surrounded by electricity quietly doing useful work. When Amina in Hargeisa switches on an LED lamp to finish her homework after Maghrib, or when Yusuf's family in a village near the Jubba River charges phones from a small solar panel and a battery, they are all relying on one big idea: electricity can only travel through certain materials, and it is stopped by others. In this unit you will learn exactly which materials let electricity flow, which ones block it, and most importantly, how this knowledge keeps you safe.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain the difference between an electrical conductor and an insulator, using the idea of moving electrons.
Test everyday household objects to find out whether they are conductors or insulators.
Describe how a simple circuit works and why it must be a complete, unbroken loop.
Draw and read simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, bulb, wire, and switch.
Predict what happens to a bulb when you add more cells or more bulbs to a circuit.
Explain, using science, why metal tools conduct electricity and why plastic or rubber handles keep you safe.
Apply electrical safety rules at home, especially the rule of never touching electricity with wet hands and never using mains electricity for experiments.
Study this table carefully. These are the words scientists use when they talk about electricity. You will meet them again and again, so learning them now will make everything easier.
A material that lets electric current flow through it easily.
Copper wire, aluminium foil, a steel spoon, salty water.

concepts.title: What Is Electricity Made Of?
concepts.explanation: To understand conductors and insulators, you first need to picture what is happening inside a material — far too small to see with your eyes. Everything around you, the wire, the wood, the water, your own hand, is made of tiny building blocks called atoms. Atoms are so small that millions of them would fit across the width of a single hair. Inside each atom there is a centre, and around that centre move even tinier particles called electrons. Electrons carry a negative electrical charge. Here is the key idea. In…
concepts.example: In other materials, such as plastic, rubber, glass and dry wood, the electrons are held very tightly to their atoms. They cannot leave and wander about. Imagine children who must stay in their assigned seats and are not…

concepts.title: Conductors vs Insulators
concepts.explanation: Now that you know the secret is moving electrons, you can understand the two great families of materials. A conductor is any material that has free electrons and therefore lets current flow through it easily. Most metals are excellent conductors: copper, aluminium, iron, steel, gold and silver. This is why the wires that carry electricity are made of metal, usually copper, because copper conducts extremely well and is not too expensive. An insulator is a material whose electrons are locked in place, so current…
concepts.example: Some materials are surprising. Graphite, the grey 'lead' inside a pencil, is not a metal, yet it conducts electricity because its carbon atoms are arranged so that some electrons can move. Water is even more…

concepts.title: How a Circuit Works
concepts.explanation: Electricity is only useful when it flows in a complete loop. This loop is called a circuit. Think of the water channels farmers dig to bring water from the Jubba or Shabelle rivers to their crops. If the channel is complete, water flows and the plants drink. If someone digs across it and breaks it, the water stops. A circuit is exactly like that, but for electricity. A simple circuit needs three things: a source to push the current (a cell or battery), a path for the current to travel along (conducting wires), and…
concepts.example: If there is a break anywhere in the loop — a loose wire, a gap, or an insulator placed in the path — the current cannot flow and the bulb goes dark. This is the whole idea behind a switch. A switch is simply a…

concepts.title: Series Circuits and Changing the Current
concepts.explanation: When you join parts one after another in a single loop, so the current has only one path to follow, you have built a series circuit. In a series circuit, the same current flows through every part, one after the other, like a line of people passing water buckets from hand to hand along a single line. If any one person leaves the line, the whole chain stops. You can change how brightly a bulb shines by changing the circuit. Here are the two most important rules, which you will test yourself with real batteries:
concepts.example: Adding more cells makes the bulb brighter. Each 1.5 V cell adds more electrical push (voltage). Two cells in series give about 3 V, which pushes the current harder, so the bulb glows more brightly. This is exactly why a…

concepts.title: Circuit Symbols and Diagrams
concepts.explanation: Scientists all over the world, from Mogadishu to Malaysia, need a quick way to draw circuits that everyone understands. Drawing a realistic picture of every wire and bulb would take too long, so instead we use simple agreed symbols. A circuit diagram is like a map: it does not look exactly like the real thing, but it shows clearly how everything is connected. Here are the symbols you must learn to recognise and draw. Because we cannot print pictures here, each one is carefully described in words so you can draw it…
concepts.example: Cell: one long thin line and one short thick line side by side. The long line is the positive (+) end, the short thick line is the negative (-) end.

concepts.title: Why Metal Tools Conduct and Plastic Handles Protect
concepts.explanation: Now let us connect the science to real life. Have you noticed that a good pair of pliers, a screwdriver, or a knife has a metal working part but a plastic or rubber handle? This is not just for comfort. The metal part conducts, and the plastic handle insulates. If the tool ever touched a live wire, the insulating handle would stop the electricity from travelling up into the person's hand. The handle is a deliberate safety barrier, designed using exactly the science you are learning. The same thinking explains why…
concepts.example: [!] Did You Know? Birds can sit safely on a single high power line without being hurt, because they are only touching one wire and electricity has no complete path through them to the ground. If the bird touched two…

explorations.title: Which Household Objects Conduct Electricity?
explorations.context: To test everyday objects from around your home and sort them into conductors and insulators.
explorations.prompt: Before you begin, write down your prediction: Which objects do you think will conduct electricity, and why? For example, 'I think the metal spoon will conduct because it is a metal.'
explorations.answer: Which objects were conductors? What do they have in common? Which objects were insulators? What do they have in common? Did any result surprise you? (Pencil graphite often surprises people.) Why? Look at the wire itself: which part is the conductor and which part is the insulator?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To test everyday objects from around your home and sort them into conductors and insulators.

explorations.title: Changing the Current with Cells and Bulbs
explorations.context: To find out how the brightness of a bulb changes when you add more cells or more bulbs in series.
explorations.prompt: Predict: What will happen to the bulb's brightness if you use two batteries instead of one? What will happen to two bulbs joined one after another?
explorations.answer: How did adding a second battery change the brightness? Explain using the idea of 'push' (voltage). How did adding a second bulb in series change the brightness? Why must the bulbs share? When you removed one of the two series bulbs, what happened to the other, and why? How does this explain why old…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how the brightness of a bulb changes when you add more cells or more bulbs in series.

explorations.title: Build a Switch from Household Materials
explorations.context: To build a working switch and show that a circuit only carries current when the loop is complete.
explorations.prompt: Predict: What must happen at the switch for the bulb to light? What happens when the switch is open?
explorations.answer: Why does the bulb light only when the paper clip touches both pins? Why did you use a metal paper clip and not a plastic one for the moving part? How is your home-made switch the same as a real light switch on the wall?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a working switch and show that a circuit only carries current when the loop is complete.

explorations.title: Does Salty Water Conduct? (Guided, Battery-Only)
explorations.context: To show that plain water conducts poorly but salty water conducts well, and to understand why this matters for safety. [SAFE] Safety First Use ONLY a 1.5 V battery. NEVER bring mains electricity anywhere near water. Only the two battery-powered wire probes…
explorations.prompt: Predict: Will the bulb light in plain water? Will it light more when you stir in salt? Why?
explorations.answer: Why did plain water barely conduct, but salty water conducted well? Salt splits into charged particles called ions when it dissolves. How do these help carry current? Your body contains water and dissolved salts. What does this experiment tell you about touching electricity with wet hands?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that plain water conducts poorly but salty water conducts well, and to understand why this matters for safety. [SAFE] Safety First Use ONLY a 1.5 V battery. NEVER bring mains electricity anywhere near water. Only the two battery-powered wire probes…

methods.title: Which Household Objects Conduct Electricity?
methods.example: To test everyday objects from around your home and sort them into conductors and insulators.
steps: Build a simple testing circuit: connect the battery to the bulb with wires, leaving two loose wire ends as testing probes.
First touch the two probes together. The bulb should light. This proves your circuit works.
Now hold one test object so it touches both probes at the same time, bridging the gap.
Watch the bulb. If it lights, the object is a conductor. If it stays dark, the object is an insulator.
Record your result, then remove the object and test the next one.

methods.title: Changing the Current with Cells and Bulbs
methods.example: To find out how the brightness of a bulb changes when you add more cells or more bulbs in series.
steps: Build a simple loop: one battery and one bulb. Note how bright it is (call this 'normal').
Add a second battery in series (line them up + to -). Observe the brightness now.
Return to one battery. Now add a second bulb in series (one after the other in the same loop). Observe both bulbs.
With two bulbs lit in series, gently unscrew or disconnect one bulb. Watch what happens to the other.
Record everything. Do not leave three batteries connected for long, as the bulb may blow.

methods.title: Build a Switch from Household Materials
methods.example: To build a working switch and show that a circuit only carries current when the loop is complete.
steps: Push two drawing pins into a piece of card, a short gap apart.
Loop one end of a paper clip around one pin so it can swing to touch the second pin.
Connect this switch into your battery-and-bulb loop, using the two pins as the switch contacts.
Swing the paper clip so it touches both pins (closed switch). Observe the bulb.
Swing it away so there is a gap (open switch). Observe again.

methods.title: Does Salty Water Conduct? (Guided, Battery-Only)
methods.example: To show that plain water conducts poorly but salty water conducts well, and to understand why this matters for safety. [SAFE] Safety First Use ONLY a 1.5 V battery. NEVER bring mains electricity anywhere near water. Only the two battery-powered wire probes…
steps: Set up your testing circuit with two loose probe ends, as in Experiment 1.
Dip the two probes into the plain water, a small gap apart (do not let them touch). Observe the bulb — it will be dark or very dim.
Stir in a spoon of salt until it dissolves. Dip the probes again. Observe the bulb.
Add a little more salt, stir, and test again. Note any change.
Observation Sheet

workedExamples.title: Comparing Brightness
workedExamples.prompt: Let us reason through a common circuit question step by step, the way a scientist would.
workedExamples.solution: Question: Aisha builds Circuit A with one 1.5 V cell and one bulb. Then she builds Circuit B with two 1.5 V cells (in series) and one identical bulb. In which circuit is the bulb brighter, and why? Step 1 — Find the total push (voltage). Circuit A has one cell, so the push is 1.5 V. Circuit B has two cells in series, so we add them: 1.5 V + 1.5 V = 3 V. Step 2 — Think about what voltage does. Voltage is the push that drives the current. More push means more current flows through the same bulb. Step 3 — Connect…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which material is the best electrical conductor? (a) plastic (b) rubber (c) copper (d) glass
workedExamples.solution: 1 (c) copper — copper is a metal with many free electrons, an excellent conductor used for wires.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: What makes metals good conductors? (a) they are heavy (b) they have free electrons (c) they are shiny (d) they are hard
workedExamples.solution: 2 (b) free electrons — metals have loosely held electrons that drift and carry current.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: A circuit must be a complete loop so that: (a) it looks neat (b) current can flow (c) the wire stays cool (d) the battery lasts longer
workedExamples.solution: 3 (b) current can flow — a break leaves a gap that stops the flow.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is an insulator? (a) steel key (b) aluminium foil (c) dry wood (d) salty water
workedExamples.solution: 4 (c) dry wood — dry wood has no free electrons; the others all conduct.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Adding a second cell in series to a single-bulb circuit will usually make the bulb: (a) dimmer (b) brighter (c) go out (d) change colour
workedExamples.solution: 5 (b) brighter — a second cell adds voltage (push), driving more current.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Two identical bulbs joined one after another in a single loop will each be: (a) brighter than one bulb (b) the same (c) dimmer than one bulb (d) off
workedExamples.solution: 6 (c) dimmer than one bulb — two bulbs in series share the same push, so each gets less.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The symbol of a circle with a cross inside it stands for a: (a) cell (b) switch (c) bulb (d) wire
workedExamples.solution: 7 (c) bulb — the circle-with-a-cross is the standard lamp/bulb symbol.

practice.prompt: Which material is the best electrical conductor? (a) plastic (b) rubber (c) copper (d) glass
practice.answer: 1 (c) copper — copper is a metal with many free electrons, an excellent conductor used for wires.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What makes metals good conductors? (a) they are heavy (b) they have free electrons (c) they are shiny (d) they are hard
practice.answer: 2 (b) free electrons — metals have loosely held electrons that drift and carry current.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A circuit must be a complete loop so that: (a) it looks neat (b) current can flow (c) the wire stays cool (d) the battery lasts longer
practice.answer: 3 (b) current can flow — a break leaves a gap that stops the flow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is an insulator? (a) steel key (b) aluminium foil (c) dry wood (d) salty water
practice.answer: 4 (c) dry wood — dry wood has no free electrons; the others all conduct.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Adding a second cell in series to a single-bulb circuit will usually make the bulb: (a) dimmer (b) brighter (c) go out (d) change colour
practice.answer: 5 (b) brighter — a second cell adds voltage (push), driving more current.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Two identical bulbs joined one after another in a single loop will each be: (a) brighter than one bulb (b) the same (c) dimmer than one bulb (d) off
practice.answer: 6 (c) dimmer than one bulb — two bulbs in series share the same push, so each gets less.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The symbol of a circle with a cross inside it stands for a: (a) cell (b) switch (c) bulb (d) wire
practice.answer: 7 (c) bulb — the circle-with-a-cross is the standard lamp/bulb symbol.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why is salty water dangerous with electricity but pure water much less so? (a) salt makes water hotter (b) salt forms ions that carry current (c) salt makes water heavier (d) salt changes the colour
practice.answer: 8 (b) salt forms ions that carry current — dissolved salt makes charged ions; pure water lacks them.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why do good tools have plastic or rubber handles? (a) for colour (b) to insulate and protect the user (c) to add weight (d) to conduct better
practice.answer: 9 (b) to insulate and protect the user — the insulating handle stops current reaching the hand.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: For safe experiments you should use: (a) a wall socket (b) a 1.5 V battery (c) a car engine (d) a power line
practice.answer: 10 (b) a 1.5 V battery — small batteries are safe; mains and engines are dangerous.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A switch turns a circuit off by: (a) adding a bulb (b) making a gap in the loop (c) adding a battery (d) using thicker wire
practice.answer: 11 (b) making a gap in the loop — opening the switch breaks the circuit.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which best explains why a bird can sit safely on one power line? (a) birds cannot feel electricity (b) there is no complete path through the bird to the ground (c) feathers are conductors (d) the wire is switched off
practice.answer: 12 (b) no complete path through the bird to the ground — touching only one wire means no loop.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Which Household Objects Conduct Electricity?
activities.materials: One 1.5 V battery (AA or similar); One small torch bulb or LED; Two or three pieces of wire (single-strand copper is easiest); A little tape to hold connections; Objects to test: metal spoon, steel key, aluminium foil, coin, plastic ruler, rubber band, dry wooden stick, dry cloth, glass marble,…
steps: Build a simple testing circuit: connect the battery to the bulb with wires, leaving two loose wire ends as testing probes.
First touch the two probes together. The bulb should light. This proves your circuit works.
Now hold one test object so it touches both probes at the same time, bridging the gap.
Watch the bulb. If it lights, the object is a conductor. If it stays dark, the object is an insulator.
Record your result, then remove the object and test the next one.
Safety: Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Changing the Current with Cells and Bulbs
activities.materials: Two or three 1.5 V batteries; Two identical small bulbs or LEDs; Connecting wires and tape
steps: Build a simple loop: one battery and one bulb. Note how bright it is (call this 'normal').
Add a second battery in series (line them up + to -). Observe the brightness now.
Return to one battery. Now add a second bulb in series (one after the other in the same loop). Observe both bulbs.
With two bulbs lit in series, gently unscrew or disconnect one bulb. Watch what happens to the other.
Record everything. Do not leave three batteries connected for long, as the bulb may blow.
Safety: Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Build a Switch from Household Materials
activities.materials: One 1.5 V battery, one bulb, wires; Two metal drawing pins or a paper clip and two pins; A small piece of card or a bottle cap as a base
steps: Push two drawing pins into a piece of card, a short gap apart.
Loop one end of a paper clip around one pin so it can swing to touch the second pin.
Connect this switch into your battery-and-bulb loop, using the two pins as the switch contacts.
Swing the paper clip so it touches both pins (closed switch). Observe the bulb.
Swing it away so there is a gap (open switch). Observe again.

Safety: Pins and nails have sharp points — ask an adult to help, and store them safely afterwards. Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Does Salty Water Conduct? (Guided, Battery-Only)
activities.materials: One 1.5 V battery, one bulb or LED, wires; A cup of plain water; A spoon of table salt; A dry cloth for spills
steps: Set up your testing circuit with two loose probe ends, as in Experiment 1.
Dip the two probes into the plain water, a small gap apart (do not let them touch). Observe the bulb — it will be dark or very dim.
Stir in a spoon of salt until it dissolves. Dip the probes again. Observe the bulb.
Add a little more salt, stir, and test again. Note any change.
Observation Sheet
Safety: Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Observe: Circuit Symbols and Diagrams
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of circuit symbols and diagrams around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Why Metal Tools Conduct and Plastic Handles Protect
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of why metal tools conduct and plastic handles protect around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: One long thin line beside one short thick line (+ is the long line).
Two or more cell symbols in a row.
A plain straight line joining parts.
A circle with a cross (X) inside it.
A gap in the line with a small lever lifted up (no current).
The lever lowered to close the gap (current flows).
Two or more cells joined for more push (voltage).
The electrical push, in volts (V). Torch = 1.5 V; mains = ~220 V.
A part that opens or closes the loop to stop or start current.
Parts joined one after another in a single loop.
A charged particle (e.g. from dissolved salt) that carries current in water.

vocabulary.example: A part that opens (breaks) or closes (completes) a circuit to turn current on or off.

vocabulary.meaning: One long thin line beside one short thick line (+ is the long line).
vocabulary.example: Draw and read simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, bulb, wire, and switch.

vocabulary.meaning: Two or more cell symbols in a row.
vocabulary.example: The small battery teaches you everything you need to understand, safely.

vocabulary.meaning: A plain straight line joining parts.
vocabulary.example: As you study, remember that every wire in your home, every plug, and every torch was designed by someone who understood these ideas.

vocabulary.meaning: A circle with a cross (X) inside it.
vocabulary.example: The same electricity that lights a bulb can hurt a person if it flows the wrong way.

vocabulary.meaning: A gap in the line with a small lever lifted up (no current).
vocabulary.example: Swing it away so there is a gap (open switch).

vocabulary.meaning: The lever lowered to close the gap (current flows).
vocabulary.example: Swing the paper clip so it touches both pins (closed switch).

vocabulary.meaning: Two or more cells joined for more push (voltage).
vocabulary.example: The small battery teaches you everything you need to understand, safely.

vocabulary.meaning: The electrical push, in volts (V). Torch = 1.5 V; mains = ~220 V.
vocabulary.example: Two or more cells joined together to give more push (voltage).

vocabulary.meaning: A part that opens or closes the loop to stop or start current.
vocabulary.example: Draw and read simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, bulb, wire, and switch.

vocabulary.meaning: Parts joined one after another in a single loop.
vocabulary.example: When you join parts one after another in a single loop, so the current has only one path to follow, you have built a series circuit.

vocabulary.meaning: A charged particle (e.g. from dissolved salt) that carries current in water.

commonMistakes: Thinking all metals conduct the same.
Thinking current gets used up in a loop.
Current is the same all around a series loop.
Pure water is a poor conductor.
Mains is dangerous and never allowed.

connections.text: a circuit transfers energy from the battery to light and heat in the bulb.
conductors and insulators are chosen for their properties, like in earlier materials topics.
conduction depends on how electrons are arranged in atoms.
torches, solar panels, phones, and home wiring all use these ideas.
knowing why wet hands are dangerous protects your whole family.
Investigate how a solar panel and battery system lights a home far from the grid.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9 (b) to insulate and protect the user — the insulating handle stops current reaching the hand.

fluency.prompt: For safe experiments you should use: (a) a wall socket (b) a 1.5 V battery (c) a car engine (d) a power line
fluency.answer: 10 (b) a 1.5 V battery — small batteries are safe; mains and engines are dangerous.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 (b) a 1.5 V battery — small batteries are safe; mains and engines are dangerous.

fluency.prompt: A switch turns a circuit off by: (a) adding a bulb (b) making a gap in the loop (c) adding a battery (d) using thicker wire
fluency.answer: 11 (b) making a gap in the loop — opening the switch breaks the circuit.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 (b) making a gap in the loop — opening the switch breaks the circuit.

fluency.prompt: Which best explains why a bird can sit safely on one power line? (a) birds cannot feel electricity (b) there is no complete path through the bird to the ground (c) feathers are conductors (d) the wire is switched off
fluency.answer: 12 (b) no complete path through the bird to the ground — touching only one wire means no loop.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 (b) no complete path through the bird to the ground — touching only one wire means no loop.

realProblems.prompt: Amina's torch is dim. She has three fresh 1.5 V batteries. What could she do to make it brighter, and what risk must she avoid?
realProblems.answer: She can add a second (or third) cell in series to raise the voltage and brighten the torch. She must avoid adding so many cells that the bulb overheats and blows, and must only ever use the small batteries — never mains.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She can add a second (or third) cell in series to raise the voltage and brighten the torch. She must avoid adding so many cells that the bulb overheats and blows, and must only ever use the small batteries — never mains.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf's family charges phones from a solar panel and a battery. Explain the job of the copper wires and the plastic coating on those wires.
realProblems.answer: The copper wires are conductors carrying the electricity from the battery to the phones; the plastic coating is an insulator that keeps the current inside the wire and the family safe.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The copper wires are conductors carrying the electricity from the battery to the phones; the plastic coating is an insulator that keeps the current inside the wire and the family safe.

realProblems.prompt: After doing the dishes, Halima wants to switch on the light. What should she do first, and why?
realProblems.answer: She should dry her hands fully first, because wet skin conducts electricity much more easily, making a shock more likely.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She should dry her hands fully first, because wet skin conducts electricity much more easily, making a shock more likely.

realProblems.prompt: Omar finds a cable in the yard with cracked plastic and bare metal showing. What should he do, and why is the bare metal dangerous?
realProblems.answer: He should not touch it and should tell a trusted adult, because the bare metal is a conductor that could carry dangerous current into anyone who touches it.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should not touch it and should tell a trusted adult, because the bare metal is a conductor that could carry dangerous current into anyone who touches it.

realProblems.prompt: Explain why you must never poke a metal fork into a wall socket, using the words conductor and mains electricity.
realProblems.answer: Metal is an excellent conductor, and mains electricity is very powerful (around 220 V). A fork in a socket would carry that dangerous electricity straight into your hand.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Metal is an excellent conductor, and mains electricity is very powerful (around 220 V). A fork in a socket would carry that dangerous electricity straight into your hand.

realProblems.prompt: Two circuits both use one bulb. Circuit X has one cell; Circuit Y has two cells in series. Predict which bulb is brighter and explain each step of your reasoning.
realProblems.answer: Circuit Y is brighter. Step 1: Y has 1.5 V + 1.5 V = 3 V; X has 1.5 V. Step 2: more voltage means more push. Step 3: more push drives more current through the bulb. Step 4: more current makes it glow brighter — so Y is brighter.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Circuit Y is brighter. Step 1: Y has 1.5 V + 1.5 V = 3 V; X has 1.5 V. Step 2: more voltage means more push. Step 3: more push drives more current through the bulb. Step 4: more current makes it glow brighter — so Y is brighter.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In your own words, explain the difference between a conductor and an insulator.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A conductor lets electric current flow through it easily because it has free electrons (e.g. metals). An insulator does not let current flow because its electrons are tightly bound (e.g. plastic, rubber).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why must a circuit be a complete loop? What happens if there is a break?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A circuit must be a complete loop so current has an unbroken path from the cell, through the parts, and back. If there is a break, the current stops everywhere and the bulb goes dark.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why adding more cells in series makes a bulb brighter.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Each cell adds more voltage (push). More push drives more current through the bulb, so it glows brighter. (Too many cells can blow the bulb.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why adding more bulbs in series makes each bulb dimmer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Bulbs in series must share the battery's push. With two bulbs, each gets only about half the share, so each is dimmer than a single bulb would be.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why do line workers wear rubber gloves and boots?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Rubber is a good insulator, so gloves and boots block electricity from flowing through the worker's body to the ground, preventing shock.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two reasons you must never touch a switch with wet hands.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Wet skin conducts far better than dry skin, and water may contain ions. Touching a switch with wet hands gives electricity an easy path through you, risking a dangerous shock.

questions.question: Which material is the best electrical conductor?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: copper — copper is a metal with many free electrons, an excellent conductor used for wires.

questions.question: What makes metals good conductors?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: free electrons — metals have loosely held electrons that drift and carry current.

questions.question: A circuit must be a complete loop so that:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: current can flow — a break leaves a gap that stops the flow.

questions.question: Which of these is an insulator?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dry wood — dry wood has no free electrons; the others all conduct.

questions.question: Adding a second cell in series to a single-bulb circuit will usually make the bulb:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: brighter — a second cell adds voltage (push), driving more current.

questions.question: Two identical bulbs joined one after another in a single loop will each be:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dimmer than one bulb — two bulbs in series share the same push, so each gets less.

questions.question: The symbol of a circle with a cross inside it stands for a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: bulb — the circle-with-a-cross is the standard lamp/bulb symbol.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: salt forms ions that carry current — dissolved salt makes charged ions; pure water lacks them.

selfAssessment: I can explain the difference between an electrical conductor and an insulator, using the idea of moving electrons.
I can test everyday household objects to find out whether they are conductors or insulators.
I can describe how a simple circuit works and why it must be a complete, unbroken loop.
I can draw and read simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, bulb, wire, and switch.
I can predict what happens to a bulb when you add more cells or more bulbs to a circuit.
I can explain, using science, why metal tools conduct electricity and why plastic or rubber handles keep you safe.
I can apply electrical safety rules at home, especially the rule of never touching electricity with wet hands and never using mains electricity for experiments.
I can study this table carefully. These are the words scientists use when they talk about electricity. You will meet them again and again, so learning them now will make everything easier.
